meta.title: Ece Dalpolat — AI Engineer & Data Scientist
meta.description: Portfolio of Ece Dalpolat, AI Engineer & Data Scientist

hero.bio: I'm a production AI engineer: I take data to models, models to secure services, and services into live products. Not demos — measurable, documented, scalable systems. Currently pursuing an MSc at Işık University.

about.label: Background
about.title: About Me
about.p1: I'm an AI Engineer and data science enthusiast pursuing a Master's in Information Technologies at Işık University, with a Bachelor's in Software Engineering from Kırklareli University. My work focuses on connecting data, models, and production systems in one flow.
about.p2: At Qkare, I own core pieces of a multi-tenant enterprise analytics stack: the dbt transformation layer (109+ models, native PostgreSQL RLS, AI precompute marts), production LangChain / LangGraph agents over behavioral data, and advisor microservices for KPIs and competency selection. I also built the agent API gateway that standardizes auth, invoke contracts, and observability across downstream AI services.
about.p3: My work spans structured LLM output (JSON mode, Pydantic validation), embedding-free and vector RAG patterns, LLM-as-judge evaluation pipelines, and cost-aware inference cascades — from early clustering R&D through production B2B SaaS.

e1.desc: Primary engineer on Qkare's B2B AI platform: dbt transformation layer (109+ models, RLS, AI precompute marts), behavioral analytics agents (LangChain ReAct, tenant-safe SQL), and advisor microservices (KPI & competency selection). Built the agent API gateway, corporate chatbot, and supporting CI/Docker infrastructure.

e2.desc: Conduct weekly lectures on data science and machine learning. Guide participants through hands-on projects, evaluate submissions, and support the learning process.

e3.desc: Assisted faculty development and taught software lab courses. Supervised students building software projects with a focus on data science and deep learning.

e4.desc: Collected and processed data across honey value chains. Developed and optimized deep learning models using Python, TensorFlow, and scikit-learn, and integrated them with external systems through APIs.

e5.desc: Developed automation tests using Selenium and TestComplete. Conducted manual testing to keep software stable, functional, and aligned with user requirements.

e6.desc: Built scalable software solutions using Java, Eclipse/IntelliJ IDEA, and SQL. Contributed to software development work in a national research environment.

e7.desc: Developed applications using ASP.NET MVC, C#, SQL Server, and Visual Studio. Strengthened team communication and collaboration skills in active software projects.

c1.title: CVAnalyzerAI — Resume Analysis Platform
c1.desc: Intelligent resume analysis platform using MCP and multiple AI services. Analyzes CVs to extract strengths, weaknesses, and job-fit insights using LLMs and structured parsing. Integrated into Claude via MCP.
c1.impact: Helps business teams reduce review time and gain AI-driven insights about candidates.

c2.title: Antenna Behaviour Analysis
c2.desc: Analyzed insect antenna movement using DeepLabCut to extract behaviour patterns from biological footage. Labeled keypoints, trained custom models, and analyzed movement trends.
c2.impact: Enabled high-accuracy behavioural studies in entomological experiments.

c3.title: Pollen Classification
c3.desc: Built a deep learning model to classify pollen images with over 90% accuracy. Created a labeled dataset, applied data augmentation, and trained CNN models.
c3.impact: Assisted agricultural researchers in automating pollen type recognition.

c4.title: Marketing Campaign Prediction
c4.desc: Prediction engine for banking marketing campaigns to forecast customer subscription behaviour. Applied feature engineering, handled class imbalance with SMOTE, and built a Streamlit interface.
c4.impact: Enabled accurate targeting for cost-effective campaign execution.

c5.title: TOP4HONEYCHAINS — Smart Agri-Food
c5.desc: ML solutions in honey value chains for traceability and quality assurance. Collected field data, trained predictive models, and created RESTful endpoints.
c5.impact: Improved transparency across agri-food stakeholders and streamlined quality tracking.

e1.desc: Specialisation in Data Science and Machine Learning. Thesis focuses on using machine learning to automate processes in beekeeping and honey extraction.

e2.desc: Foundation in software development, algorithms, database systems, and software testing methodologies.

contact.label: Get in touch
contact.title: Contact
contact.intro: I'm open to new opportunities, collaborations, and interesting conversations about AI, data engineering, and ML. Feel free to reach out through any of the channels below.

analytics.title: Analytics Platform
analytics.sub: Qkare · Nov 2025 – May 2026 · Multi-tenant HR Analytics Stack
analytics.desc: End-to-end analytics platform for talent management — ingesting complex behavioral and cognitive data via PostgreSQL FDW, transforming it through a 3-layer dbt pipeline, and serving embedded Superset dashboards with per-tenant, per-user Row-Level Security. Primary owner of the dbt transformation layer, responsible for modeling, security, and the live delivery path.
analytics.highlightDesc: My core responsibility was turning fragmented assessment data into a production-grade decision layer. I standardized AON, Saville, and Assessment Center scores, built the mart structure, enforced tenant isolation with native RLS, and stabilized the end-to-end dashboard flow. Platform scaffolding (K8s, CI, guest-token API) was built in parallel by the team; my focus was data modeling, security, and delivery consistency.

c1.title: Problem
c1.desc: The problem was fragmentation: assessment data lived in separate systems, and non-technical teams could not reliably turn it into fast, trustworthy insights. Without a single decision layer, reporting and interpretation stayed inconsistent.

c2.title: My contribution
c2.desc: I consolidated the data into one analytics backbone: the dbt transformation layer, canonical score normalization, AI precompute marts, and reusable fact/seed structures for reporting. That made the same dataset consistent across both analysis and dashboarding.

c3.title: Impact
c3.desc: Native RLS made tenant isolation reliable and enabled secure reporting at 100+ model scale. It removed the dependency issues that were blocking production and directly unlocked live dashboard usage.

c4.title: Solution
c4.desc: I packaged the FDW → dbt → Superset loop so it could run with a single command: local DB provisioning, quick-start scripts, schema comparison tools, and mock-source integration to speed up development.

c5.title: Multi-tenant API & UAT deploy
c5.desc: Led the multi-tenancy refactor, UAT analytics-api configuration & Dockerfile, tenant-level GitLab CI security tests, and RLS alignment between the API and dbt layers.

c6.title: Production unblock & TR localisation
c6.desc: Fixed schema drift (candidate_user) blocking 130+ downstream models before a live demo; stabilised Superset Docker startup; updated Turkish UI translations and filter localisation (tr-TR).

clustering.title: Behavioral Clustering R&D
clustering.sub: Qkare · Aug 2025 · Employee segmentation & talent analytics
clustering.desc: Research workspace for segmenting employees and candidates from behavioral and performance data — comparing K-Means, hierarchical clustering, DBSCAN, and GMM on the same feature matrix, with PCA/t-SNE visualization and silhouette / Calinski-Harabasz / Davies-Bouldin evaluation. Sole author — I built the full pipeline from exploration to model comparison.
clustering.highlightDesc: My main job in this R&D track was to make raw behavioral data usable for comparative segmentation. I combined feature engineering, multi-algorithm benchmarking, dimensionality reduction, and segment profiling into one offline analytics flow. The outputs fed the mart structure and AI precompute layers in the Analytics Platform.

c1.title: Problem
c1.desc: The problem was fragmentation: behavioral data was split across sources, and there was no fair way to compare different clustering methods on the same dataset. That made segmentation decisions hard to explain, hard to reproduce, and hard to trust.

c2.title: My contribution
c2.desc: I built the feature engineering pipeline, scaling, joblib serialization, and multi-algorithm benchmark flow. That made K-Means, hierarchical, DBSCAN, and GMM directly comparable on one dataset and tied the analysis to a single experiment backbone.

c3.title: Impact
c3.desc: PCA and t-SNE visualizations made segment separation visible, while silhouette, Calinski-Harabasz, and Davies-Bouldin scores made the selection process objective. The segment profiles became decision-ready outputs instead of just charts.

c4.title: Solution
c4.desc: I combined exploration, modeling, and reporting into one script-based flow: EDA modules, dimensionality reduction, benchmark tools, and CSV/HTML output layers. That made the research reproducible and portable into the wider platform.

coach.title: Coach Platform
coach.sub: Qkare · Sep 2025 – Nov 2025 · Multi-Agent AI Coaching
coach.desc: B2B corporate AI coaching platform — multi-agent orchestration with LangGraph, modular POML prompts, Parquet-grounded personalization, real-time voice via OpenAI Realtime API, and HR analytics dashboards. I owned the evaluation framework, the early voice stack, and the POML foundation.
coach.highlightTitle: My contribution — evaluation and voice layer
coach.highlightDesc: My main task in this project was to make the coach testable and measurable before production rollout. I built the Referee Agent for session quality scoring, shipped the first OpenAI Realtime voice path, and established the POML prompt structure. Analytics and performance-data integration moved in parallel by the team; my focus was quality assurance and interaction plumbing.

c1.title: Problem
c1.desc: The problem was that coaching flows depended too much on manual review, and neither the voice experience nor the prompt structure could be trusted before production. That made product behavior difficult to measure.

c2.title: My contribution
c2.desc: I built the LLM-as-Judge pipeline with the Referee Agent, combining the AI Client Simulator, multi-turn Coach integration, and the 5-dimension evaluation structure. I also made the POML prompt architecture and the voice path work together.

c3.title: Impact
c3.desc: The evaluation automation made session quality measurable, and the client simulator produced shorter, more consistent test flows. The real-time voice layer also turned the coach from a text-only tool into an interactive product.

c4.title: Solution
c4.desc: I separated the pattern engine, POML migration, voice bridge, and memory persistence into modular components. That made the coach easier to test and easier to extend in production.

c5.title: Client simulator & eval tooling
c5.desc: Improved AI client simulator with turn-based progression guides and anti-repetition rules (49% fewer turns, score 7.88→8.2); added coaching evaluation reports and single-scenario test scripts.

c6.title: Contributor onboarding
c6.desc: Wrote comprehensive setup guide for new contributors (submodule init, Python 3.13+, dependency fixes) and expanded Referee Agent README with scenarios, token costs, and result JSON examples.

analyzer.title: Analyzer Platform
analyzer.sub: Qkare · Apr 2025 – May 2026 · Behavioral Analytics AI Agent
analyzer.desc: Corporate behavioral analytics platform — a LangChain ReAct agent that answers natural-language business intelligence queries over domain-specific behavioral data (Saville, 360°, 9-box, competency profiles) via dbt mart tables with native PostgreSQL RLS. Dual-service architecture: Analyzer (analysis engine) + Analyzer API (voice/text conversation layer). Lead engineer on security architecture, config bootstrap, and service integration.
analyzer.highlightDesc: Primary engineer on the security and configuration foundation that made production deployment possible: merged JWT + RLS + guard pipeline into main, built the bootstrap config system agents run on, and wired observability across both analyzer engine and conversation API. ReAct agent skills, company analytics chat, and enum refactors were developed in parallel by the team; my focus was tenant-safe data access, prompt architecture, and making the dual-service stack production-ready.

c1.title: 4-layer security & RLS pipeline
c1.desc: Shipped the full RLS-security branch (33 commits): JWT auth on HTTP + WebSocket, UserContext via ContextVar, execute_rls on all SQL paths, InputGuard/OutputGuard with YAML-driven threat patterns, cross-tenant isolation guard, and QueryValidationPipeline before every LLM call.

c2.title: Config bootstrap & schema layer
c2.desc: Introduced backend/core/bootstrap/ (ConfigPathResolver, agent_config, mod_loaders); consolidated application/schema; moved position_models and security types; eliminated duplicate config packages and hardcoded prompt strings.

c3.title: Master prompt & agent reliability
c3.desc: Fixed master prompt configurations rendering (dict sections → block strings), build_master_prompt() guards, PostgreSQL TCP keepalive + stale-connection retry, SQL alias ambiguity fixes, and chat history trimming for context overflow.

c4.title: Domain mods & analysis fixes
c4.desc: Fixed FourHProfileRiskMod runtime TypeError, resolved module initialization circular import via PEP 562 lazy loading, unified SwotPatternType enums, and enhanced ProfileTransformer for employee_user_id identity validation.

c5.title: Voice layer integration
c5.desc: Langfuse trace propagation across analyzer client and chat API caller; fail-fast startup validation for analyzer service URL; global 500 error logging; improved employee ID extraction from nested survey payloads.

c6.title: Langfuse tracing
c6.desc: Integrated Langfuse callbacks in AnalizciAgent and WebSocket endpoints for end-to-end LLM trace propagation; docker-compose config and utility module for observability across the analysis pipeline.

kpi.title: KPI Advisor
kpi.sub: Qkare · Apr 2026 – May 2026 · Position-based KPI Recommendation AI
kpi.desc: AI microservice that recommends SMART-aligned KPIs for any job position — grounded in the APQC Process Classification Framework (lexical + category-prior retrieval, no vector DB), calibrated via a corporate Golden Dataset, and validated through an offline LLM-as-judge pipeline with human-in-the-loop feedback. Primary engineer on the generation engine, retrieval layer, eval pipeline, and production integration.
kpi.highlightTitle: Core ownership — generation engine & quality flywheel
kpi.highlightDesc: Primary engineer on the KPI recommendation stack: from the initial APQC-grounded generation service through Redis caching, few-shot calibration, and the four-step offline eval pipeline. Observability refactor, Streamlit UI polish, and extended prompt rules were developed in parallel by the team; my focus was structured output quality, embedding-free retrieval, dataset flywheel, and shipping production-ready CI/CD.

c1.title: KPI generation service & APQC retrieval
c1.desc: Built the core FastAPI service: OpenAI JSON-mode structured output, hybrid lexical + category-prior retrieval over 1631 PCF nodes and 2680 metrics, diversity selection, confidence-aware fallback, and prompt assembly with domain-expert persona and corporate rules.

c2.title: Retrieval optimization & prompt engineering
c2.desc: Pre-tokenized lexical similarity for latency; modularized APQC constants and schemas; industry-specific standards in prompts (DORA, LCR/EVE, CAC/LTV); few-shot selection from Golden Dataset xlsx; Redis SHA-256 cache with graceful degradation.

c3.title: Offline quality pipeline
c3.desc: Implemented step2 LLM-as-judge eval (SMART ×0.35 + Relevance ×0.40 + Clarity ×0.25), step3 Excel export for human review, and step4 feedback import into golden dataset — closing the synthetic data → eval → human flywheel loop.

c4.title: Schema evolution & backward compatibility
c4.desc: Refactored PositionInput with LeadershipLevel and PositionGroup models; model_validator accepts legacy flat payloads alongside canonical nested format; optional ID field; detailed OpenAI usage logging per request.

c5.title: CI/CD, testing & Docker
c5.desc: GitLab CI pipeline with pre-commit (Ruff, MyPy), pytest + coverage, smoke tests for schema integrity; multi-stage Docker with healthchecks; catalog-mode position sourcing for pipeline bootstrap.

c6.title: CORE gateway integration
c6.desc: Wired Streamlit UI to the agent gateway via /api/v1/agents/ga-kpi-advisor/invoke with {input}/{output} envelope, dev auth bypass headers, and monorepo endpoint configuration for local and UAT flows.

skills.title: Skills Advisor
skills.sub: Qkare · Aug 2025 – May 2026 · AI competency dimension selection
skills.desc: AI advisor that selects exactly 12 competency dimensions from a closed dictionary for any job title — supporting Ocean and Saville frameworks, with Turkish rationales for domain experts. I led the founding product, closed-dictionary pipeline, and caching strategy.
skills.highlightDesc: The goal here was to make 12-dimension selection reliable, explainable, and cheap. I designed the closed-dictionary data layer, Turkish rationales, caching strategy, and both inference paths. O*NET integration and observability moved in parallel by the team.

c1.title: Problem
c1.desc: The problem was that competency dimensions for a role were often chosen subjectively. Existing processes were inconsistent and weak at producing Turkish explanations.

c2.title: My contribution
c2.desc: I built the closed-dictionary data layer, served the dimension mappings through an API, and injected context into LLM prompts. I also implemented caching, validation, and repeatable selection logic.

c3.title: Impact
c3.desc: I moved competency selection out of subjective judgment and into a measurable workflow. That made the same job title produce consistent, explainable, Turkish rationale-backed dimension recommendations.

c4.title: Solution
c4.desc: I implemented two inference paths: single-pass JSON mode and a DSPy expert ensemble. That gave the selection engine flexibility across speed, explainability, and cost.

c5.title: Multi-layer caching
c5.desc: SHA256 LLM-level cache in core LLM processing modules plus request-level cache in LangChainService (May 2026); switched default to gpt-4o-mini @ temperature 0 for cost/latency — identical role+test+group requests skip OpenAI entirely.

c6.title: Production integration
c6.desc: JWT + tenant whitelist via grid-auth; OTEL and prompt logging hooks; Docker netrc secrets for private PyPI; pairs with Agent API Gateway invoke path for downstream enterprise workflows.

gateway.title: Agent API Gateway
gateway.sub: Qkare · Apr 2026 – May 2026 · Multi-agent AI API Monorepo
gateway.desc: Corporate AI agent gateway — a FastAPI monorepo that exposes multiple downstream AI microservices through one standard invoke API: shared JWT auth, request/response envelope, structured logging, and OpenTelemetry hooks. I led platform bootstrap, auth integration, Docker topology, and dynamic CI.
gateway.highlightTitle: My contribution — platform contract and deployment
gateway.highlightDesc: My main task was to make the shared entry point for the agent ecosystem production-ready. I built the monorepo layout, first agent wiring, JWT auth, per-agent Docker/compose pattern, and dynamic CI generation. The rest of the team iterated on create_gateway_app, CORS, and scaffolding improvements in parallel; my focus was the contract and deployment topology.

c1.title: Problem
c1.desc: The problem was that each agent integration created repeated boilerplate and rising maintenance cost without a shared auth and deployment pattern. New services needed a common entry point.

c2.title: My contribution
c2.desc: I built the shared gateway core: router factory, invoke schemas, JWT verification, per-agent container structure, and dynamic CI generation. That made the base scaffolding manageable from one place.

c3.title: Impact
c3.desc: The gateway reduced integration cost by unifying different AI services behind one standard invoke flow. With the container and auth contract clear, teams could ship agents faster.

c4.title: Solution
c4.desc: I designed per-agent Docker Compose, dynamic GitLab child pipelines, and one invoke contract. That turned the gateway into a low-friction platform for new services.

c5.title: First production agent integration
c5.desc: Wired ga-kpi-advisor as the reference agent: httpx proxy to downstream KPI service, env-based URL config, validation error logging, and end-to-end invoke flow from Streamlit through the gateway.

c6.title: Cross-service contract alignment
c6.desc: Kept gateway payloads in sync with KPI Advisor schema evolution: leadershipLevel, positionGroup, name/definition fields, optional id — ensuring monorepo invoke examples match the downstream Pydantic models.

chatbot.title: QKare AI Assistant
chatbot.desc: FastAPI chatbot for the QKare corporate website — answers visitor questions through a cost-controlled cascade: rule-based matching first, then semantic cache (FAISS or Milvus), then live content RAG, and finally OpenAI for open-ended replies. I rewrote it end to end, including architecture and Docker deployment.
chatbot.highlightDesc: My main task in this rewrite was to build a production-ready, security-first chatbot backbone. I shipped hybrid security, dual vector-store support, and the four-layer inference cascade. The semantic cache and RAG patterns established here were reused in later AI services.

c1.title: Problem
c1.desc: The problem was that visitor questions needed fast answers, but calling the LLM directly was expensive and the site content changed often. A static FAQ alone was not enough.

c2.title: My contribution
c2.desc: I built the rule → semantic cache → RAG → LLM flow. I also designed the cache services, FAISS/Milvus options, and the retrieval layer grounded in live site context.

c3.title: Impact
c3.desc: This setup reduced cost for repeat questions, kept answers aligned with current site content, and added a security layer against prompt injection. The product reached the level of a true corporate website assistant.

c4.title: Solution
c4.desc: I implemented hybrid security modules, configurable prompt-injection thresholds, and production packaging. That turned the chatbot into both an economical and secure service.

c5.title: Production packaging
c5.desc: Organized services/ layout (qa, cache, milvus, rag, llm); Docker Compose for Milvus + Attu admin UI; structured logging, custom exceptions, /health endpoint, and env-driven config validation in centralized configuration modules.
